label: Brian Quintenz
role: Kalshi Board Member (Nov 2021); a16z crypto Global Head of Policy (Oct 2021-Feb 2025); CFTC Chair nominee (withdrawn Sept 2025)
bio: The tightest loop in the graph. CFTC Commissioner 2017-2021 -> simultaneously joined Kalshi's board (Nov 2021) AND became a16z crypto's Global Head of Policy (Oct 2021) -- a16z being Kalshi's lead investor. In early 2025 Trump nominated him to chair the CFTC, the agency that regulates a16z's portfolio company Kalshi. His transition team reportedly sought confidential CFTC information about Kalshi's competitors (Polymarket, PredictIt), triggering conflict-of-interest accusations. The White House withdrew his nomination on September 30, 2025 after a public dispute with the Winklevoss twins (Gemini) and mounting congressional pressure. Michael Selig was then confirmed as CFTC Chair in December 2025. Kalshi called him a perfect fit for regulatory integrity (2021).

label:
Judge Jia Cobb
(D.D.C.)
role: U.S. District Judge (D.D.C.); granted summary judgment for Kalshi, vacating the CFTC's election-contract ban (Sept 2024)
bio: U.S. District Judge for the District of Columbia (confirmed 2021). In KalshiEX LLC v. CFTC she granted summary judgment for Kalshi in September 2024, vacating the CFTC order that had blocked Kalshi's congressional-control (election) contracts. Rejecting the agency's theory that the contracts were 'gaming' or unlawful activity, she wrote: 'Kalshi's contracts do not involve unlawful activity or gaming. They involve elections, which are neither.' She held the CFTC lacked the broad authority it claimed, reasoning its logic would let it prohibit virtually any event contract.

sources.text: Davis Wright Tremaine - District Court Vacates CFTC Prohibition on 'Congressional Control Contracts' (Oct 2024), quoting Judge Cobb
KalshiEX LLC v. CFTC docket (CourtListener)

verification: Verified: ruling and quoted language per Davis Wright Tremaine analysis and the court docket (D.D.C., Sept 2024).

label:
Judge Patricia Millett
(D.C. Circuit)
role: U.S. Circuit Judge (D.C. Circuit); authored the per curiam order denying the CFTC's emergency stay (Oct 2, 2024)
bio: U.S. Court of Appeals judge for the D.C. Circuit. On a per curiam panel with Judges Cornelia Pillard and Florence Pan, she authored the court's October 2, 2024 order denying the CFTC's emergency motion to stay Judge Cobb's ruling pending appeal. The panel held the CFTC had not shown the 'public will suffer irreparable injury absent a stay pending appeal,' dissolving the administrative stay and letting Kalshi's election markets go live before the November 2024 election.

verification: Verified: panel (Millett, Pillard, Pan), per curiam stay denial, and quoted 'irreparable injury' language per the D.C. Circuit order (No. 24-5205) and Lowenstein Sandler client alert (Oct 2024). label:
State Gaming
Regulators (NV/NJ/MD+)
role: State gaming regulators (Nevada GCB, NJ DGE, Maryland, and others) - issued cease-and-desist orders against Kalshi's sports event contracts (2025-26)
bio: A coalition of state gaming regulators that issued cease-and-desist orders against Kalshi after it launched sports event contracts in 2025, arguing the contracts are unlicensed sports betting under state law. Kalshi countered that the Commodity Exchange Act gives the CFTC exclusive jurisdiction over its federally regulated contracts, preempting state gambling law. The dispute split the federal courts: New Jersey and (initially) Nevada enjoined the states for Kalshi, while Maryland sided with the regulators and Nevada later reversed. By 2026 the fight had expanded to the Ninth Circuit and many more states (per CourtListener dockets).

sources.text: Holland & Knight - New Jersey Federal Court Sides with Kalshi Over Prediction Market Contracts (May 2025)
Mondaq - Disagreement Deepens Among Federal Courts Over Whether Sports Event Contracts Are Subject to State Regulation
CourtListener - Kalshi docket search (multi-state suits, 2025-26)

verification: Verified: multi-state cease-and-desist wave and federal court split (NJ/NV initially for Kalshi; MD against; NV reversed) per Holland & Knight and Mondaq analyses; 2025-26 docket expansion per CourtListener.

label:
Judge Edward Kiel
(D.N.J.)
role: U.S. District Judge (D.N.J.); granted Kalshi a preliminary injunction against New Jersey gaming regulators (April 2025)
bio: U.S. District Judge for New Jersey. In April 2025 he granted Kalshi a TRO and preliminary injunction against the New Jersey Division of Gaming Enforcement, which had issued a cease-and-desist over Kalshi's sports event contracts. Crediting Kalshi's federal-preemption theory, he wrote that 'Kalshi's sports-related event contracts evidence - by their very existence - the CFTC's exercise of its discretion and implicit decision to permit them.' A leading pro-Kalshi data point in the state-preemption split.

label:
Judge Andrew Gordon
(D. Nev.)
role: U.S. District Judge (D. Nev.); granted then DISSOLVED Kalshi's preliminary injunction, ruling sports contracts fall under state gaming law (Nov 2025)
bio: U.S. District Judge for Nevada. He first granted Kalshi a preliminary injunction in April 2025, but reversed course on November 24, 2025, dissolving it and holding that Kalshi's sports-related contracts 'closely resemble' traditional sportsbook bets and fall within Nevada's gaming laws. He wrote that Kalshi 'relies on a strained reading of the already convoluted CEA in an attempt to evade state regulation,' an interpretation that would 'upset decades of federalism regarding gaming regulation' and is 'contrary to Congress' intent.' A leading anti-Kalshi data point that deepened the federal court split.

verification: Verified: April 2025 PI grant then Nov 24, 2025 dissolution, with quoted language, per iGaming Business, National Law Review, and The Nevada Independent.

label:
Keith Ellison
(MN Attorney General)
role: Minnesota AG; defends the first U.S. felony ban on prediction markets - sued by both Kalshi and the CFTC (2026)
bio: Minnesota Attorney General. In May 2026 Minnesota became the first state to criminalize prediction markets outright (a felony ban effective Aug 1, 2026). Kalshi sued Ellison and Governor Tim Walz on May 28, 2026, arguing the ban violates the Supremacy Clause and the CFTC's exclusive jurisdiction under the Commodity Exchange Act; the CFTC separately sued Minnesota on May 19, 2026 (as it also did Illinois, New York, Connecticut and Arizona). Polymarket filed its own suit. Ellison defends the ban, saying prediction markets are 'designed to be addictive and prey especially on young people and low-income folks.'

label:
Ninth Circuit
Court of Appeals
role: U.S. Court of Appeals (9th Cir.); denied Kalshi & Polymarket stays (May 2026), letting Nevada & Washington enforcement proceed
bio: On May 22, 2026, the Ninth Circuit denied Kalshi's and Polymarket's bids to stay state gambling enforcement in Nevada and Washington, finding neither had shown irreparable harm or a strong likelihood of success on its federal-preemption argument. The ruling let the state cases proceed and stands as a key appellate counterweight to the 2024 D.C. Circuit decision, deepening the split among federal courts over whether the CEA preempts state gaming law for sports event contracts.

label:
Tribal Gaming
(NM Pueblos & Apache)
role: New Mexico tribes (Mescalero Apache + Pojoaque, Sandia, Isleta Pueblos) - sued Kalshi alleging IGRA / tribal-sovereignty violations (May 2026)
bio: On May 12, 2026, four New Mexico tribes and pueblos (the Mescalero Apache Tribe with the Pojoaque, Sandia and Isleta Pueblos) sued Kalshi, alleging its sports event contracts are illegal sports betting on tribal lands that violate the Indian Gaming Regulatory Act (IGRA), tribal-state compacts, and age rules (18 vs 21), depriving tribes of compact revenue that funds schools and services. They argue the Commodity Exchange Act does not override IGRA, which makes no reference to tribal authority. It was the third tribal suit against Kalshi (after California and Wisconsin); New Mexico AG Raul Torrez separately sued Kalshi on June 4, 2026.

label: Fairshake PAC
role: Crypto-industry super PAC; $133M spent on 2024 U.S. elections; top backers: Coinbase ($74M), Ripple ($73M), a16z ($70M)
bio: The most powerful corporate PAC in the 2024 U.S. elections, spending $133M total. Three co-equal backers: Coinbase ($74M), Ripple ($73M), a16z ($70M). Helped elect 53 pro-crypto members of Congress and Trump to the presidency. Directly links Kalshi's lead investor (a16z), its newest distribution partner (Coinbase), and Ripple's Garlinghouse to the political-spending machine that shaped the CFTC's deregulatory pivot. Already raised $103M for 2026 midterms (same three backers). The three Fairshake pillars are now all tied to Kalshi: a16z (lead investor), Coinbase (distributor + USDC/Custody), Ripple (SEC case dropped by Atkins who mentored the new CFTC Chair Selig).

(2) Infrastructure: Coinbase Custody secures Kalshi's USDC reserves (announced Nov 2025); Kalshi prediction markets run on USDC. (3) Political: $74M co-backer of Fairshake PAC in the 2024 cycle -- the same PAC funded by a16z ($70M, Kalshi lead VC) and Ripple ($73M, whose SEC case was dropped by Paul Atkins, mentor to current CFTC Chair Selig). The distributor IS the PAC co-funder.

label:
J. Christopher
Giancarlo
role: "Crypto Dad"; CFTC Chair (2017-2019); Polymarket advisor (2022); Crypto.com amicus brief; mentor to current CFTC Chair Selig
bio: Former CFTC Chair (2017-2019, Trump-appointed), earned the nickname 'Crypto Dad' for overseeing the world's first federally regulated Bitcoin futures markets. After leaving the CFTC, Giancarlo became a full-spectrum advocate for the crypto/prediction-market ecosystem: he took an advisory role at Polymarket (2022), co-authored an amicus brief supporting Crypto.com against Nevada gaming regulators, and mentored Michael Selig (current CFTC Chair) during Selig's clerkship. His successor-chain now runs the CFTC. In April 2026 he left law to become a full-time crypto advisory practitioner.

label: Caroline Pham
role: CFTC Acting Chair (Jan 20 - Dec 22, 2025); executed enforcement pivot dropping ~half of open CFTC matters
bio: Designated Acting CFTC Chair by Trump on January 20, 2025. In under a year she: (1) dropped approximately half of all open CFTC enforcement matters; (2) explicitly reshaped regulated prediction markets (Prediction News: 'Five Ways Caroline Pham Reshaped Regulated Prediction Markets'); (3) enabled PredictIt to finally receive its full DCM license. The enforcement actions dropped under her tenure include those against Kalshi's competitors Polymarket and Crypto.com. She departed the CFTC after Michael Selig was confirmed as Chair on December 22, 2025.

label: Michael Selig
role: CFTC Chair (Dec 22, 2025-); clerked for Giancarlo; was Paul Atkins's senior SEC advisor; withdrew anti-prediction-market rules
bio: Current CFTC Chair (sworn in December 22, 2025, confirmed 18-2). Career mentor-chain: law clerk to Giancarlo as CFTC Commissioner → private practice advising CFTC registrants → Chief Counsel to SEC Crypto Task Force → Senior Advisor to SEC Chair Paul Atkins → CFTC Chair. This means the current CFTC Chair was mentored by 'Crypto Dad' Giancarlo (who now advises Polymarket) and was Paul Atkins's right hand at the SEC (the same Atkins who dropped charges enabling Ripple's return to Fairshake PAC fundraising). On January 29, 2026 Selig withdrew the CFTC's 2024 proposed rulemaking that would have restricted prediction markets, and withdrew the 2025 advisory against sports event contracts -- clearing the regulatory path for Kalshi's expansion.

label: Paul Atkins
role: SEC Chair (Apr 2025-); dropped Coinbase/Binance/Ripple/Robinhood enforcement; paused Justin Sun case; mentored Michael Selig
bio: SEC Chair from April 2025, replacing Gary Gensler. Under Atkins: crypto enforcement fell 60%; the SEC dropped cases against Coinbase, Binance, Ripple, Kraken, and Robinhood; the Justin Sun/Tron case was paused. He then named Michael Selig (his senior advisor at the SEC Crypto Task Force) to be CFTC Chair. On Jan 29, 2026 Atkins and Selig co-hosted a rare joint 'Project Crypto' SEC-CFTC summit. Atkins's enforcement pullback and his mentorship of the current CFTC Chair form a direct institutional pathway from the SEC to the CFTC's current prediction-market stance.

label:
Brad Garlinghouse
(Ripple CEO)
role: Ripple CEO; $73M Fairshake backer (2024) + $25M pledged for 2026; Ripple SEC case dropped under Atkins
bio: CEO of Ripple (XRP issuer). Third co-equal backer of Fairshake PAC: $73M in the 2024 cycle; $25M more pledged for 2026 midterms. The SEC's long-running case against Ripple was dropped under Paul Atkins -- who then mentored the new CFTC Chair Selig. Garlinghouse credited Fairshake with 'absolutely supercharging' the 2024 results. XRP rose 300%+ after the election. This connects the same political-spending network backing Kalshi's VC (a16z) and distribution partner (Coinbase) to a case of personal financial gain flowing from the PAC's political investment.

sources.text: CoinTelegraph: Ripple contributes another $25M to Fairshake PAC before US midterms
CNBC: Coinbase, a16z and others pour more than $78M into pro-crypto PAC for 2026 election
Cryptopolitan: SEC Chair Paul Atkins defends pausing Justin Sun case

verification: Verified: $73M Fairshake 2024 per CNBC/CoinTelegraph; $25M 2026 pledge per CoinTelegraph; Ripple SEC case dropped per CoinDesk/The Block.

label: David Sacks
role: White House AI & Crypto Czar (Jan 2025-Mar 2026); PayPal Mafia; held Coinbase + Robinhood shares before divestment
bio: Trump's special advisor on AI and crypto (Jan 2025-Mar 2026; 130-day SGE limit). PayPal Mafia member alongside Peter Thiel (Polymarket backer) and Elon Musk. His OGE disclosure revealed a $200M+ crypto portfolio including direct Coinbase shares and Robinhood shares -- both Kalshi distribution partners -- which he divested before taking office. Retained minor crypto positions through Craft Ventures. The White House granted him a limited ethics waiver despite retained holdings. Democrats launched an ethics investigation. He shaped the regulatory environment (CFTC/SEC enforcement pivots) under which Kalshi, Polymarket, Crypto.com, and Justin Sun all saw enforcement actions dropped or paused. After his 130 days he joined the President's Council of Advisers on Science & Technology.

sources.text: White House statement: Appointment of David O. Sacks as 'White House A.I. & Crypto Czar'
NBC New York: David Sacks sold $200M in crypto-related holdings before taking White House job, ethics memo says
SFist: Trump's Silicon Valley AI and Crypto Czar David Sacks Apparently Enriching Himself and Friends In White House Role (Dec 2025)
CNBC: David Sacks says his time as Trump's crypto and AI czar has ended (Mar 2026)

verification: Verified: White House role per official White House statement; Coinbase/Robinhood divestment per OGE ethics memo / NBC New York; PayPal Mafia per Wikipedia.
